title: Are Movies to Blame?
gist: A review of David Thomson's 'A Sudden Flicker of Light' argues cinema molded audiences toward a rotten media-saturated present culminating in Trump-as-'movie man,' a thesis the reviewer finds compelling in its media-continuity argument but overstated.

insights.claim: Film historian David Thomson argues the movie screen is both a window to imagined reality and a mirror that molds and even seduces the audience that watches it.
insights.speaker: Jordan Hoffman
insights.so_what: A frame for how visual media shapes mass psychology—relevant to attention-economy and media-influence theses.

insights.claim: Thomson draws a continuous line from early 'actuality' films of Edison and the Lumières to today's never-ending scroll of shareable 'content,' implying cinema built toward media saturation.
insights.speaker: Jordan Hoffman
insights.so_what: Positions today's short-form content economy as the endpoint of a 150-year arc—context for platform/attention dynamics.

insights.claim: Thomson depicts Donald Trump as 'our movie man' and the logical endpoint of a culture that celebrated charismatic antiheroes like Charles Foster Kane and Michael Corleone.
insights.speaker: Jordan Hoffman
insights.so_what: Argues mass media manufactures the political strongman—a media-and-power narrative worth noting.

insights.claim: The reviewer disagrees that everything from The Wizard of Oz to Parasite was a cursed stopover leading to devastating media saturation, arguing the pessimistic media-decline thesis is overstated.
insights.speaker: Jordan Hoffman
insights.so_what: A counter to techno-cultural declinism—useful skepticism toward 'media ruined us' narratives.

insights.claim: Thomson's history largely omits international cinema, Black American filmmakers, and video games, weakening its claim to comprehensive revisionism.
insights.speaker: Jordan Hoffman
insights.so_what: Notes the analytic blind spots in a canonical-Western-film framing of cultural history.

insights.claim: D.W. Griffith's Birth of a Nation was pivotal to the artistic and business development of movies yet also effectively revived the Ku Klux Klan—illustrating cinema's power to reshape society for ill.
insights.speaker: Jordan Hoffman
insights.so_what: Historical case that mass media can directly mobilize social/political movements at scale.
